seoTitle: How to Use Inbox in Trello
metaDescription: Manage your Trello notifications with the unified Inbox panel, filtering mentions, archiving read items, and following key cards.
intro: Cross-functional project updates can bury important feedback under a pile of notifications if you're not using the right tools. This guide covers navigating Trello's unified Inbox to filter mentions, clear read items, and keep track of the cards that matter most.

steps.heading: Open your Inbox
steps.body: From your dashboard, find and open your central profile communication desk.

steps.heading: Review your notification feed
steps.body: Scan the clustered list of board events and team updates gathered into one clean stream.

steps.heading: Filter by mentions
steps.body: Use the filtering controls to separate direct @mentions from general board activity.

steps.heading: Mark items as read or archive them
steps.body: Use single-click actions to clear notifications you've already handled.

steps.heading: Adjust notification preferences
steps.body: Update your account settings to prevent duplicate alerts from also arriving in your external email.

steps.heading: Follow important cards
steps.body: Use the Follow feature on high-priority cards to stream focused updates without wading through unrelated activity.

faq.question: How do I separate mentions from general updates?
faq.answer: The Inbox includes filtering controls that let you isolate direct @mentions from broader board activity logs.

faq.question: Can I stop getting duplicate email alerts?
faq.answer: Yes, adjusting your account notification preferences can prevent messages from arriving in both Trello and your external email.

faq.question: How do I keep track of one important card without watching the whole board?
faq.answer: Use the Follow utility on that specific card to stream high-priority updates directly to your Inbox.

faq.question: What happens when I archive a notification?
faq.answer: Archiving removes it from your active feed, helping you keep your Inbox focused on what still needs attention.
